rationale: The cited paper is used in the core theorizing, not just in a background citation list. The citing article explicitly adopts Zhu and Meyers-Levy's embodied vs referential meaning distinction to frame how audio stimuli can shape judgments, and then builds its hypotheses around timbral sound quality in audio logos. It also explicitly positions its contribution as extending that prior notion of embodied musical meaning to abstract brand personality inferences.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on Zhu and Meyers-Levy's music-meaning framework and extends it to timbral properties of audio logos. It tests whether timbral roughness/smoothness can elicit embodied meaning that shifts ruggedness/sophistication brand personality perceptions.
